Name: spectral_precision_routing
Title: Spectral Precision Routing: Training-Free Precision Allocation via Activation Covariance Geometry
Short Hypothesis: We hypothesize that the local covariance condition number and spectral decay rate of hidden states serve as direct proxies for quantization sensitivity, enabling training-free precision allocation via spectral quantization routing.
Related Work: Our work builds upon recent advances in mixed-precision inference, spectral quantization, and training-free optimization. Unlike existing methods that rely on static profiling or costly calibration, our approach dynamically allocates numerical precision based on real-time activation geometry, providing a transparent and reproducible pathway for resource-efficient inference under natural distribution shifts.
Abstract: We propose Spectral Precision Routing (SPR), a training-free framework that dynamically allocates numerical precision based on real-time activation geometry. By computing local covariance condition numbers and spectral decay rates of hidden states, we derive a precision-switching policy that enforces strict latency budgets. We theoretically ground SPR in PAC-Bayes bounds, linking spectral conditioning to generalization stability under quantization noise. Evaluated on single-GPU setups across WILDS benchmarks, SPR achieves a superior throughput-accuracy Pareto frontier compared to static and heuristic mixed-precision baselines.
Experiments: Evaluate SPR on WILDS benchmarks, comparing its performance to static and heuristic mixed-precision baselines.
Conduct ablation studies to investigate the impact of spectral decay rate and covariance condition number on precision allocation.
Assess the robustness of SPR to distributional shifts and noisy inputs, measuring its ability to maintain accuracy and latency targets.
Risk Factors and Limitations: The effectiveness of SPR may depend on the quality of the activation geometry and the choice of spectral quantization parameters.
The computational overhead of SPR may be higher than that of static mixed-precision methods due to the need for real-time covariance computation.
The theoretical guarantees provided by SPR may not hold in practice due to the complexity of real-world datasets and models.
